when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order for a patient has a matching naloxone prescription, the agent must first search for all active opioid analgesic orders; for each active opioid found, it should check if a naloxone prescription exists, and if any active opioid lacks naloxone, create a naloxone order—do not conclude that no naloxone order is needed unless it is explicitly confirmed there are truly no active opioid orders.

when asked to ensure a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must first check for any existing active orders—if there are zero, create one; if there are multiple, discontinue all but the newest; if exactly one exists, do nothing and do not create a new order. The main mistake was failing to actually create the new order when none were present.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 or 12 hours, the agent must explicitly search for and retrieve all heart rate measurements within each timeframe and compute the average using any values found; do not report "no measurements available" unless you have checked and confirmed there are truly no data points in those periods.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 or 12 hours, the agent must retrieve all heart rate measurements within each specified timeframe and compute the average using all available values, rather than reporting a single value or using the same value for both periods if multiple measurements are present.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 or 12 hours, the agent must explicitly search for and retrieve all heart rate measurements within each specified timeframe and compute the average using all available values found; do not report only a single value or use the same value for both periods if multiple measurements are present.

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen and order a new one if it was done more than 12 months ago, the agent must first check if any prior CT Abdomen procedures exist; if none are found, it should order a new CT Abdomen as indicated, but if a prior study exists, only order a new one if the most recent was performed more than 12 months ago by accurately comparing the procedure date to the current date. The main mistake is failing to check for the absence of prior studies before deciding "no new order needed."

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order for a patient has a matching naloxone prescription, the agent must first search for all active opioid analgesic orders; for each active opioid found, check if a naloxone prescription exists, and if any active opioid lacks naloxone, create a naloxone order—do not conclude that no naloxone order is needed unless it is explicitly confirmed there are truly no active opioid orders.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 or 12 hours, the agent must explicitly search for and retrieve all heart rate measurements within each specified timeframe and compute the average using any available values found; do not report "no measurements available" unless you have checked and confirmed there are truly no data points in those periods.

when asked to determine the date of the last influenza vaccine and order a new one if it was administered more than 365 days ago, the agent must correctly compare the last vaccine date to today and only order if more than 365 days have elapsed (i.e., strictly greater than 365 days); do not order if it has been exactly 365 days or less.

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen and order a new study if it was performed more than 12 months ago, the agent must first check if any prior CT Abdomen procedures exist; if none are found, it must order a new CT Abdomen as indicated, but if a prior study exists, only order a new one if the most recent was performed more than 12 months ago by accurately comparing the procedure date to the current date. The main mistake was not confirming whether any prior CT Abdomen studies exist before deciding "no new order needed."

when asked to check if a urinary catheter has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, the agent must explicitly confirm both (1) that the catheter’s insertion time is over 48 hours ago and (2) that there is no documented removal order or removal event, by thoroughly searching all relevant insertion and removal records; only create a service request if both conditions are definitively verified, rather than assuming based on incomplete or limited information.

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order for a patient has a matching naloxone prescription, the agent must first search for all active opioid analgesic orders; for each active opioid found, it must check if a naloxone prescription exists, and if any active opioid lacks naloxone, create a naloxone order—do not conclude that "no naloxone order is needed" unless it is explicitly confirmed there are truly no active opioid orders.

when asked to ensure a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must first check for any existing active orders—if there are zero, create one; if multiple, discontinue all but the newest; if exactly one exists, do nothing and do not create a new order. The main mistake was failing to actually create the new order when none were present.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 or 12 hours, always explicitly search for and retrieve all heart rate measurements within each specified timeframe and compute the average using any available values found; do not report "no measurements available" unless you have checked and confirmed there are truly no data points in those periods.

when asked to ensure a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must first check for any existing active orders—if there are zero, create one; if there are multiple, discontinue all but the newest; if exactly one exists, do nothing and do not create or discontinue any orders. The main mistake was acting (discontinuing) when only two active orders were present, instead of only intervening when there are zero or more than one.

when asked to ensure a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must first check for existing active orders—if there are zero, create one; if there are multiple, discontinue all but the newest; if exactly one exists, do nothing and do not create a new order. The main mistake was failing to create a new order when none were present.

when asked to ensure a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must first check for any existing active orders—if there are zero, it must create a new order; the main mistake was failing to actually create the new order when none were present.
